title: Energy flow through ecosystems
description: Test your knowledge of energy flow through ecosystems!

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows the internal body temperature of a polar bear during various activities.

Polar bear activity | Internal body temperature
:-: | :-:
Resting in a shallow pit in the snow | $37 \degree\text{C}$
Swimming in cold water | $38 \degree\text{C}$
Walking across an icy landscape | $38 \degree\text{C}$
Hunting near a hole in the ice | $37 \degree\text{C}$

**Which of the following statements is most likely true about the polar bear described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The polar bear’s body temperature remains relatively constant due to the thermal energy generated by its metabolism.
The polar bear’s body temperature increases significantly as its muscles use energy for walking or swimming.
The polar bear is endothermic, carrying out certain behaviors in order to maintain a constant body temperature.
The polar bear is ectothermic, using internal mechanisms to maintain its body temperature within a given range.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows the internal body temperature of a hippopotamus during various activities.

Hippopotamus activity | Internal body temperature
:-: | :-:
Basking in the sun on a riverbank | $36 \degree\text{C}$
Resting submerged in deep water | $36 \degree\text{C}$
Climbing a steep riverbank | $37 \degree\text{C}$
Fighting another hippopotamus for territory | $37 \degree\text{C}$

**Which of the following statements is most likely true about the hippopotamus described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The hippopotamus’s body temperature remains relatively constant due to the thermal energy generated by its metabolism.
The hippopotamus’s body temperature increases significantly as its muscles use energy for climbing or fighting.
The hippopotamus is endothermic, carrying out certain behaviors in order to maintain a constant body temperature.
The hippopotamus is ectothermic, using internal mechanisms to maintain its body temperature within a given range.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows the internal body temperature of a black rat snake during  activities carried out in different environmental conditions.

Black rat snake activity | Internal body temperature
:-: | :-:
Coiled on a rock in the sun | $27 \degree\text{C}$
Resting inside a barn | $26 \degree\text{C}$
Moving through a forest during the day | $23 \degree\text{C}$
Moving through a forest at night | $20 \degree\text{C}$

**Which of the following statements is most likely true about the black rat snake described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The black rat snake is ectothermic, so its body temperature changes substantially depending on the temperature of its surroundings.
The black rat snake is endothermic, carrying out certain behaviors in order to maintain a constant body temperature.
The black rat snake’s body temperature remains relatively stable whether it’s at rest in the sun, or using its muscles to move across the forest floor.
The black rat snake primarily uses thermal energy generated by its own metabolism to increase its body temperature.

assessment_items.question_content:
Hydrothermal vents are openings in the sea floor that release hot, mineral-rich water. These vents are found in areas with high volcanic activity, and are so deep in the ocean that no sunlight can reach them. 

Hydrothermal vents help create a thriving ecosystem by releasing certain inorganic compounds into the surrounding area. Bacteria harness energy from these compounds, producing sugar molecules that they can use as food. The bacteria provide a food source for giant tube worms, mussels, and shrimp, which in turn are eaten by eel-like fish. 

**Which of the following describes the bacteria in a deep sea hydrothermal vent ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The bacteria are autotrophs, because they use chemical energy from inorganic compounds to produce their own food.
The bacteria are autotrophs, because they use photosynthesis to produce sugar molecules that they can use as food.
The bacteria are heterotrophs, because they provide a food source to other organisms such as giant tube worms, mussels, and shrimp.
The bacteria are heterotrophs, because they harness energy by consuming other organisms that live near the hydrothermal vents.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Sundarbans is a coastal area of India and Bangladesh that contains the world’s largest mangrove forest ecosystem. The dominant mangrove tree in the Sunderbans, *Heritiera fomes*, grows in the salty coastal water, sending aerial roots up above the water’s surface to help get oxygen. 

The roots of the mangrove trees provide food and habitat for many species of invertebrates and fish, which provide a food source for various snakes and birds. These animals, in turn, are eaten by the Sundarban’s top predator, the Bengal tiger.

**Which of the following best describes *H. fomes* in the Sundarbans mangrove forest ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *H. fomes* is an autotroph, because it uses photosynthesis to produce sugar molecules that it can use as food.
*H. fomes* is an autotroph, because it uses chemical energy from inorganic salts to produce its own food.
*H. fomes* is a heterotroph, because it provides food for various species of invertebrates and fish.
*H. fomes* is a heterotroph, because it harnesses energy from dead organisms decomposing near its roots.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Marshall Islands are a remote group of islands in the Pacific Ocean, halfway between Hawaii and Australia. The Marshall Islands include multiple atolls, or ring-shaped islands consisting of coral reefs and a central lagoon. The coral reef ecosystem of the Marshall Islands is rich with biodiversity.

In this ecosystem, microscopic phytoplankton carry out photosynthesis, using energy from sunlight to make sugars. The phytoplankton provide a food source for other microscopic organisms called zooplankton. The zooplankton are consumed by various bony fish and manta rays, which in turn provide a food source for sharks.

**Based on the evidence provided in the text, which of the following best describes the zooplankton in the Marshall Islands coral reef ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The zooplankton are heterotrophs, because they harness energy by consuming other organisms in the coral reef ecosystem.
The zooplankton are heterotrophs, because they provide a food source for other organisms like bony fish and manta rays.
The zooplankton are autotrophs, because their microscopic size allows them to use chemical energy from inorganic compounds to produce their own food.
The zooplankton are autotrophs, because they use photosynthesis to produce sugar molecules that they can use as food.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows the food sources for various organisms in a marine ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following food webs best represents the movement of matter and energy among the organisms described in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Arrows in food web diagrams point in the direction of matter and energy transfer. So, arrows point *away* from the organisms being eaten, and *toward* the organisms doing the eating.

The table tells us which organisms are eaten by, or are food sources for, which other organisms in the marine ecosystem. We can use this information to determine the correct food web diagram. 

For example, according to the table, kelp is a food source for kelp bass. This means that the correct food web diagram will have an arrow pointing away from kelp, toward kelp bass:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that:

- phytoplankton are a food source for zooplankton and toadfish, so there will be arrows pointing from phytoplankton toward zooplankton and toadfish,
- toadfish are a food source for kelp bass, so there will be an arrow pointing from toadfish toward kelp bass,
- and zooplankton are a food source for kelp bass and toadfish, so there will be arrows pointing from zooplankton toward kelp bass and toadfish.

options.alt: A chart showing various organisms with their food sources. Kelp and phytoplankton have no food source listed. The food sources of kelp bass are kelp, toadfish, and zooplankton. The food sources of toadfish are phytoplankton and zooplankton. The food source of zooplankton is phytoplankton.

choices.content: ![A food web. Arrows point from kelp, zooplankton, and toadfish to kelp bass. Arrows point from zooplankton and phytoplankton to toadfish. An arrow points from phytoplankton to zooplankton.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e34d8ef7917e037d92a841fa8433f899.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from kelp bass to kelp, zooplankton, and toadfish. Arrows point from toadfish to zooplankton and phytoplankton. An arrow point from zooplankton to phytoplankton.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/43ff2711faf9be1fc44d27db618042fd.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from kelp and zooplankton to kelp bass. An arrow points from kelp to zooplankton. Arrows point from zooplankton and phytoplankton to toadfish.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9820445428f0bc0ea4b0ca76c75bdde9.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from kelp bass to kelp and zooplankton. Arrows point from toadfish to zooplankton and phytoplankton. An arrow point from zooplankton to kelp.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4f2797ec03936e0fc92f1f84900f35f9.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows the food sources for various organisms in a desert ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following food webs best represents the movement of matter and energy among the organisms described in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Arrows in food web diagrams point in the direction of matter and energy transfer. So, arrows point *away* from the organisms being eaten, and *toward* the organisms doing the eating.

The table tells us which organisms are eaten by, or are food sources for, which other organisms in the desert ecosystem. We can use this information to determine the correct food web diagram. 

For example, according to the table, desert woodrats are a food source for rattlesnakes. This means that the correct food web diagram will have an arrow pointing away from desert woodrat, toward rattlesnake:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that:

- the creosote bush is a food source for desert woodrats and grasshoppers, so there will be arrows pointing from creosote bush toward desert woodrat and grasshopper,
- grasshopper mice are a food source for rattlesnakes, so there will be an arrow pointing from grasshopper mouse toward rattlesnake,
- and grasshoppers are a food source for grasshopper mice, so there will be an arrow pointing from grasshopper toward grasshopper mouse.

options.alt: A chart showing various organisms with their food sources. Creosote bush has no food source listed. The food source of desert woodrat is creosote bush. The food source of grasshopper mouse is grasshopper. The food source of grasshopper is creosote bush. The food sources of rattlesnake are desert woodrat and grasshopper mouse.

choices.content: ![A food web. Arrows point from creosote bush to desert woodrat and grasshopper. An arrow points from desert woodrat to rattlesnake. An arrow points from grasshopper to grasshopper mouse. An arrow points from grasshopper mouse to rattlesnake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/706e9cb8db5204526a7b269295cf4918.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from rattlesnake to desert woodrat and grasshopper mouse. An arrow points from desert woodrat to creosote bush. An arrow points from grasshopper mouse to grasshopper. An arrow points from grasshopper to creosote bush.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/11568e301a31108bcee9d39acee49619.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from creosote bush to desert woodrat and grasshopper. An arrow points from desert woodrat to rattlesnake. An arrow points from grasshopper to rattlesnake. An arrow points from grasshopper mouse to rattlesnake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/da9845b27e3a084f02a3e6eabbe0f2a5.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from rattlesnake to desert woodrat, grasshopper mouse, and grasshopper. An arrow points from desert woodrat to creosote bush. An arrow points from grasshopper to creosote bush.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/50fe92b6c30a4ed52da546b52814ae0a.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows the food sources for various organisms in a forest ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following food webs best represents the movement of matter and energy among the organisms described in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Arrows in food web diagrams point in the direction of matter and energy transfer. So, arrows point *away* from the organisms being eaten, and *toward* the organisms doing the eating.

The table tells us which organisms are eaten by, or are food sources for, which other organisms in the forest ecosystem. We can use this information to determine the correct food web diagram. 

For example, according to the table, persimmon trees provide a food source for swallowtail caterpillars and pine voles. This means that the correct food web diagram will have arrows pointing away from persimmon tree, toward swallowtail caterpillar and pine vole:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that:

- gray foxes are a food source for bobcats, so there will be an arrow pointing from gray fox toward bobcat,
- swallowtail caterpillars are a food source for gray foxes and pine voles, so there will be arrows pointing from swallowtail caterpillar toward gray fox and pine vole,
- and pine voles are a food source for gray foxes, so there will be an arrow pointing from pine vole toward gray fox.

options.alt: A chart showing various organisms with their food sources. Persimmon tree has no food source listed. The food sources of gray fox are swallowtail caterpillar and pine vole. The food source of swallowtail caterpillar is persimmon tree. The food source of bobcat is gray fox. The food sources of pine vole are persimmon tree and swallowtail caterpillar.

choices.content: ![A food web. Arrows point from persimmon tree to swallowtail caterpillar and pine vole. Arrows point from swallowtail caterpillar to pine vole and gray fox. An arrow points from pine vole to gray fox. An arrow points from gray fox to bobcat.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a1fa80f2ea819e481a1fef85cf66432a.svg)

![A food web. An arrow points from bobcat to gray fox. Arrows point from gray fox to swallowtail caterpillar and pine vole. An arrow points from swallowtail caterpillar to persimmon tree. Arrows point from pine vole to swallowtail caterpillar and persimmon tree.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b8714471a821839212795016582174e.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from persimmon tree to swallowtail caterpillar and pine vole. An arrow point from swallowtail caterpillar to bobcat. Arrows point from pine vole to swallowtail caterpillar and gray fox. An arrow points from gray fox to bobcat.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/444e2d69f5e365439300f31c2ba26610.svg)

![A food web. Arrows point from bobcat to gray fox and swallowtail caterpillar. Arrows point from swallowtail caterpillar to persimmon tree and pine vole. An arrow points from gray fox to pine vole. An arrow points from pine vole to persimmon tree.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b9f61ff1777c0aa428ed3b20974aa55f.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows the approximate dry mass (in grams per square meter) of organisms at each trophic level in a forest ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best predicts the effect of a decrease in the primary producers’ exposure to sunlight?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
Diagram of approximate dry mass of organisms at each trophic level in an ecosystem, showing 4 trophic levels. Each level is represented as a rectangle of a different size, with the widest rectangle at the bottom. The rectangles get shorter with each increasing trophic level.

Starting at the bottom, labeled primary producers (with a drawing of strawberry plants), have a dry mass of one thousand gram per square meter. The level above, labeled Primary consumers (with a drawing of a grasshopper), have a dry mass of one hundred gram per square meter. The next level up, labeled Secondary consumers (with a drawing of a bird) have a dry mass of ten gram per square meter. The top level, labeled Tertiary consumers (with a drawing of a coyote), have a dry mass of one gram per square meter.

choices.content: The biomass at all trophic levels in the forest ecosystem will decrease.
The biomass of primary producers and primary consumers will increase, but the biomass of secondary and tertiary consumers will remain the same.
The biomass of primary producers will decrease, but the other trophic levels’ biomass will remain the same.
The biomass at all trophic levels in the forest ecosystem will increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows the approximate dry mass (in grams per square meter) of organisms at each trophic level in a forest ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best predicts the effect of an increase in the primary producers’ exposure to sunlight?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
Diagram of approximate dry mass of organisms at each trophic level in an ecosystem, showing 4 trophic levels. Each level is represented as a rectangle of a different size, with the widest rectangle at the bottom. The rectangles get shorter with each increasing trophic level.

Starting at the bottom, labeled primary producers (with a drawing of leaves), have a dry mass of one thousand gram per square meter. The level above, labeled Primary consumers (with a drawing of a rabbit). have a dry mass of one hundred gram per square meter. The next level up labeled, Secondary consumers (with a drawing of a snake), have a dry mass of ten gram per square meter. The top level, labeled Tertiary consumers (with a drawing of a lynx), have a dry mass of one gram per square meter.

choices.content: The biomass at all trophic levels in the forest ecosystem will increase.
The biomass of primary producers and primary consumers will decrease, but the biomass of secondary and tertiary consumers will remain the same.
The biomass of primary producers will increase, but the other trophic levels’ biomass will remain the same.
The biomass at all trophic levels in the forest ecosystem will decrease.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows the approximate dry mass (in grams per square meter) of organisms at each trophic level in a forest ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best predicts the effect of a decrease in the primary producers’ exposure to sunlight?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
Diagram of approximate dry mass of organisms at each trophic level in an ecosystem, showing 4 trophic levels. Each level is represented as a rectangle of a different size, with the widest rectangle at the bottom. The rectangles get shorter with each increasing trophic level.

Starting at the bottom level, labeled primary producers (with a drawing of grass), have a dry mass of one thousand gram per square meter. The level above that, labeled Primary consumers (with a drawing of a grasshopper), have a dry mass of one hundred gram per square meter. The next level up, labeled Secondary consumers (with a drawing of a mouse), have a dry mass of ten gram per square meter. The top level, labeled Tertiary consumers (with a drawing of an owl), have a dry mass of one gram per square meter.

choices.content: The biomass of primary producers will decrease, and so will the biomass of the consumers.
The biomass of primary producers and primary consumers will decrease, but the biomass of secondary and tertiary consumers will remain the same.
The biomass of primary producers will increase, but the biomass of the consumers will decrease.
The biomass of primary producers will increase, and so will the biomass of the consumers.
